what-is-ai.prompt: What best describes how most of today's AI systems work?

choices.text: They follow a fixed set of hand-written rules for every situation.
choices.feedback: That's classic rule-based software. Modern AI learns patterns from data instead of being told every rule.

choices.text: They learn statistical patterns from large amounts of example data.
choices.feedback: Right — today's AI is pattern-matching at scale, not hand-coded logic.

choices.text: They are conscious and understand meaning the way people do.
choices.feedback: No — they model patterns in data. There's no understanding or awareness behind the output.

what-is-ai.prompt: A model that writes fluent text must therefore understand what it's saying. True?

choices.text: True — fluency requires comprehension.
choices.feedback: Fluency comes from predicting likely next tokens. A model can be confidently, fluently wrong.

choices.text: False — fluency is pattern prediction, not understanding.
choices.feedback: Exactly. Sounding right and being right are different things.

data-and-datasets.prompt: Why does "garbage in, garbage out" matter so much for AI?

choices.text: Models mostly inherit the patterns — and flaws — of the data they're trained on.
choices.feedback: Yes. A model is a mirror of its training data, biases and gaps included.

choices.text: Because more data always makes a model worse.
choices.feedback: Not quantity itself — it's the quality and representativeness of the data that matter.

choices.text: Because models ignore their training data once deployed.
choices.feedback: They don't — everything they 'know' was shaped by that data.

statistics-essentials.prompt: An AI predicts a 70% chance of rain. What does that 70% best represent?

choices.text: It will definitely rain on 70% of the map.
choices.feedback: Probability isn't area — it's a degree of confidence about the outcome.

choices.text: A measure of the model's confidence, not a guarantee.
choices.feedback: Right — it's an estimate of likelihood, and roughly 30% of such days it should be dry.

choices.text: The model is 70% finished computing the answer.
choices.feedback: Probability describes the prediction, not how far along the computation is.

probability-and-uncertainty.prompt: A model gives an answer with high confidence. Can it still be wrong?

choices.text: No — high confidence means it's correct.
choices.feedback: Confidence is the model's own estimate; it can be confidently, fluently wrong.

choices.text: Yes — confidence and correctness are different things.
choices.feedback: Exactly. Whether confidence actually matches accuracy (calibration) is its own hard problem.

correlation-vs-causation.prompt: A model notices ice-cream sales and drownings rise together. What's the safe conclusion?

choices.text: Ice cream causes drowning.
choices.feedback: The classic trap. A shared cause — hot weather — drives both.

choices.text: They're correlated, but one may not cause the other.
choices.feedback: Right — correlation is a pattern; causation needs more than co-occurrence.

choices.text: Drowning causes ice-cream sales.
choices.feedback: Reversing the arrow doesn't fix it — correlation alone can't tell you direction.

patterns-and-predictions.prompt: At its core, generating text from a language model is closest to which task?

choices.text: Looking the answer up in a stored database of facts.
choices.feedback: There's no lookup table — it predicts, it doesn't retrieve (unless you bolt retrieval on).

choices.text: Predicting the next most-likely piece of text, step by step.
choices.feedback: Yes — it's sequential next-token prediction, one piece at a time.

choices.text: Running a formal logical proof to derive the answer.
choices.feedback: It approximates reasoning through patterns; it isn't executing formal logic.

choices.text: A set of learned numbers (parameters) that map inputs to outputs.
choices.feedback: Right — training tunes those weights; the model is those numbers plus its architecture.

choices.text: A physical robot or server.
choices.feedback: Hardware runs the model, but the model itself is the learned parameters.

choices.text: A copy of the internet stored word-for-word.
choices.feedback: It doesn't store text verbatim — it compresses patterns into weights.

bias-in-data.prompt: Where does most harmful bias in AI systems originate?

choices.text: Patterns and imbalances in the training data and its labels.
choices.feedback: Yes — models absorb the skews of their data and how it was labelled.

choices.text: Random electrical noise in the hardware.
choices.feedback: Bias is systematic, not random noise — it traces back to data and design choices.

choices.text: The user's phrasing, and nothing else.
choices.feedback: Phrasing can surface bias, but the bias was learned from data first.

features-and-labels.prompt: In supervised learning, what is a "label"?

choices.text: The correct answer the model is trained to predict.
choices.feedback: Right — features are the inputs, the label is the target answer.

choices.text: The name of the dataset file.
choices.feedback: That's just a filename — a label is the ground-truth output for each example.

choices.text: A tag the model invents entirely on its own.
choices.feedback: Labels are supplied by humans or a process; inventing them is unsupervised territory.

training-vs-testing.prompt: Why hold out a separate test set the model never trains on?

choices.text: To check whether it generalises, not just memorises.
choices.feedback: Exactly — testing on training data flatters the model and hides overfitting.

choices.text: To give the model more data to learn from.
choices.feedback: The test set is deliberately withheld — learning from it defeats its purpose.

choices.text: Because testing is faster than training.
choices.feedback: Speed isn't the point — an honest estimate of real-world performance is.

overfitting-underfitting.prompt: A model scores 99% on its training data but 60% on new data. What's happening?

choices.text: Underfitting — the model is too simple.
choices.feedback: Underfitting scores poorly on both. The big gap points the other way.

choices.text: Overfitting — it memorised the training set instead of generalising.
choices.feedback: Right — a large train/test gap is the signature of overfitting.

choices.text: Perfect generalisation.
choices.feedback: Generalisation means close scores on both; a 39-point gap is the opposite.

neural-networks.prompt: What does a single artificial "neuron" actually do?

choices.text: Stores one fact, like a row in a spreadsheet.
choices.feedback: Neurons don't store facts — they compute a weighted sum and pass it through a function.

choices.text: Takes weighted inputs, sums them, and applies an activation function.
choices.feedback: Yes — stack millions of these and the network learns patterns.

choices.text: Makes the final yes/no decision for the whole network.
choices.feedback: At most that's the output layer's job; one neuron is a tiny building block.

gradient-descent.prompt: Gradient descent improves a model by…

choices.text: randomly guessing weights until one happens to work.
choices.feedback: It's not random — the gradient points toward weights that reduce the error.

choices.text: nudging weights in the direction that lowers the loss, step by step.
choices.feedback: Right — follow the slope downhill on the loss surface, repeatedly.

choices.text: deleting the worst training examples.
choices.feedback: It adjusts the weights, not the dataset.

tokenization.prompt: Before a language model reads your text, what happens first?

choices.text: It's split into tokens — chunks like words or word-pieces — mapped to numbers.
choices.feedback: Yes — models operate on token IDs, not raw letters.

choices.text: It's translated into English.
choices.feedback: There's no translation step — it's tokenized in whatever language it's in.

choices.text: It's checked and corrected for spelling.
choices.feedback: No spell-check stage — the text is simply tokenized.

tokenization.prompt: Why can a common word and a rare word cost different numbers of tokens?

choices.text: Rare or unusual words get split into several sub-word pieces.
choices.feedback: Right — frequent words are often one token; rare ones fragment into many.

choices.text: Longer words are always exactly one token.
choices.feedback: Length doesn't guarantee one token — rare strings split into pieces.

choices.text: Tokens are counted one per character, always.
choices.feedback: Most modern tokenizers use sub-words, not single characters.

choices.text: A list of numbers (a vector) that captures the meaning of a piece of text.
choices.feedback: Yes — similar meanings land near each other in that vector space.

choices.text: A compressed image file.
choices.feedback: Embeddings are numeric vectors for meaning, not an image format.

choices.text: The raw text, stored exactly as written.
choices.feedback: It's a learned numeric representation, not the original text.

embeddings.prompt: In embedding space, "king" and "queen" end up close together because…

choices.text: they're spelled similarly.
choices.feedback: Spelling isn't it — 'king' and 'kindle' look alike but mean different things.

choices.text: they appear in similar contexts, so they get similar vectors.
choices.feedback: Right — meaning comes from usage patterns, captured as nearby vectors.

choices.text: they have the same number of letters.
choices.feedback: Letter count has nothing to do with embeddings.

transformers.prompt: What key idea made transformers so effective for language?

choices.text: An attention mechanism that weighs the relationships between all tokens.
choices.feedback: Yes — attention lets each token look at every other token in context.

choices.text: Reading text strictly one word at a time, like older RNNs.
choices.feedback: That's the older approach transformers replaced; attention sees the whole context at once.

choices.text: Storing the entire internet to look answers up.
choices.feedback: No lookup store — they learn weighted patterns through attention.

transformers.prompt: In a transformer, "attention" lets a token…

choices.text: decide which other tokens matter most for interpreting it.
choices.feedback: Right — attention assigns learned weights to other tokens' relevance.

choices.text: pay exactly equal attention to every token, always.
choices.feedback: The whole point is unequal, learned weighting — not uniform attention.

choices.text: ignore all of the other tokens.
choices.feedback: The opposite — attention is about relating tokens to each other.

attention.prompt: In "The trophy didn't fit in the suitcase because it was too big", attention helps the model figure out…

choices.text: what "it" refers to, by weighing the surrounding words.
choices.feedback: Exactly — attention links 'it' to 'trophy' using the context.

choices.text: the correct spelling of "suitcase".
choices.feedback: Attention is about relationships in meaning, not spelling.

choices.text: nothing — pronouns are skipped entirely.
choices.feedback: Resolving references like this is precisely what attention is good at.

choices.text: The maximum amount of text it can consider at once.
choices.feedback: Right — beyond that limit, earlier content falls out of view.

choices.text: The size of your browser window.
choices.feedback: It's a token limit inside the model, not anything on your screen.

choices.text: How long the model spent training.
choices.feedback: That's training time; the context window is about input length at inference.

temperature-sampling.prompt: Raising the "temperature" when generating text does what?

choices.text: Makes the output more random and varied.
choices.feedback: Yes — higher temperature flattens the odds, so less-likely tokens appear more often.

choices.text: Makes the hardware physically run hotter.
choices.feedback: It's a sampling parameter, not hardware heat.

choices.text: Guarantees more factual answers.
choices.feedback: Higher temperature usually means less predictable output, not more accurate.

prompt-engineering.prompt: Why does giving a model an example or two in the prompt often help?

choices.text: It demonstrates the format and task, steering the next prediction.
choices.feedback: Right — in-context examples shape what the model produces next.

choices.text: It permanently retrains the model's weights.
choices.feedback: Prompting doesn't change weights — the effect lasts only for that request.

choices.text: It makes the model run faster.
choices.feedback: Examples affect quality and format, not speed — they can even cost more tokens.

rag.prompt: What problem does Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) mainly solve?

choices.text: Giving the model relevant, up-to-date facts it wasn't trained on.
choices.feedback: Yes — retrieve documents, then let the model answer grounded in them.

choices.text: Making the model retrain itself overnight.
choices.feedback: RAG adds context at query time; it doesn't retrain anything.

choices.text: Removing the need for a model at all.
choices.feedback: RAG feeds a model — it doesn't replace one.

rag.prompt: In RAG, where do the retrieved documents go?

choices.text: Into the model's prompt/context for that single answer.
choices.feedback: Right — they're injected as context, not baked into the weights.

choices.text: Permanently into the model's parameters.
choices.feedback: Nothing is written to the weights — retrieval happens per request.

choices.text: They replace the user's question.
choices.feedback: They supplement the question as supporting context, not replace it.

hallucination.prompt: Why do language models sometimes "hallucinate" confident but false statements?

choices.text: They generate plausible-sounding text, and plausible isn't always true.
choices.feedback: Exactly — they optimise for likely continuations, not verified facts.

choices.text: Someone manually typed the wrong answer in.
choices.feedback: No human typed it — the model produced a fluent guess.

choices.text: They always know when they're unsure and say so.
choices.feedback: They often don't — confidence and correctness can come apart.
